title: Check yourself
questions.0.q: You wrote two lines, one under the other, inside the same rule. The first works. The second doesn't. The browser says nothing — no error, no red. Where do you look first?
questions.0.options.0: At the second line's property name — it's probably misspelled
questions.0.options.1: At the second line's value — it's probably invalid
questions.0.options.2: At the END of the line above it — a missing semicolon kills both
questions.0.options.3: Nowhere — the second line's property just isn't supported
questions.0.hint: A line that doesn't work isn't always broken on its own. Sometimes it dies with its neighbour.
questions.0.explain: The semicolon ends a declaration. Without it the browser reads the next line as a continuation of the same value and throws both away. Then it seeks forward to the next semicolon or the closing brace and carries on from there. So exactly two lines die, not everything below — and that's why the second one looks broken when the broken one is the first. Look at the line ABOVE the problem, not at the problem.
questions.1.q: A colleague tells you your code is wrong because you wrote color: #00008b instead of color: darkblue. Who's right?
questions.1.options.0: The colleague — colour names are the only reliable way
questions.1.options.1: You — that's the same colour written differently
questions.1.options.2: The colleague — hex only works with background-color
questions.1.options.3: You're both wrong — it has to be rgb()
questions.1.hint: Ask what actually reaches the browser. The characters you typed, or the colour they mean?
questions.1.explain: darkblue, #00008b and rgb(0, 0, 139) are the same value. The browser understands them identically and stores them identically — ask it what the colour is and all three return rgb(0, 0, 139). A course that rejects one of them is comparing characters instead of results. You write for the browser, not for someone counting letters.
questions.2.q: You put the <style> block at the end of <body>. The page looks exactly as you want. Is there a problem?
questions.2.options.0: No — it works, so it's fine
questions.2.options.1: Yes — the browser won't run it there at all
questions.2.options.2: Yes — the rules will only apply to the elements BELOW the block
questions.2.options.3: Yes — on a slow connection people see the page unstyled first, then repainted
questions.2.hint: It works on your machine, in milliseconds. Think about someone on a slower connection than yours.
questions.2.explain: The browser is forgiving and will run the styles anywhere, for the whole document — where the block sits doesn't narrow their reach. The problem isn't whether it works but WHEN they arrive: placed at the end, they come after the page has already been painted, and the browser recomputes the layout and paints again. Separately from that, by the spec <style> belongs in the head, and an HTML validator will flag it as an error. This is your first lesson of the kind "the code works, it looks right, and it's still wrong". There will be more.
questions.3.q: You removed the underline from every link. The page looks cleaner and everything works. What did you break?
questions.3.options.0: Someone who can't tell colours apart can no longer see what's a link
questions.3.options.1: Nothing — the links are still clickable
questions.3.options.3: Links will stop changing colour once visited
questions.3.hint: Ask what the underline was actually doing. Was it decoration, or was it saying something?
questions.3.explain: Blue and underlined are two separate signals for the same thing. Remove the underline and only the colour is left — and around eight percent of men don't distinguish colours well. For them the link becomes ordinary text. Removing it isn't forbidden. Removing it without giving another signal is: a bottom border, a different weight, an arrow. Take something away, owe a replacement.
